exemplar.title:
How can hotel managers at Hampton by Hilton Poznan effectively
motivate employees to consistently perform at their highest
potential?

detailedExemplarData.title:
How can hotel managers at Hampton by Hilton Poznan effectively
motivate employees to consistently perform at their highest
potential?

subject.title: Business Management
subject.description:
**IB Business Management** is a **Group 3** subject in the **International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP)**. It explores **business fundamentals, human resource management, finance and accounts, marketing, and operations management**, with a strong focus on **case study analysis, real-world business applications, and strategic decision-making**.

The course is available at **Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours)** and **Higher Level (HL, 240 teaching hours)**, with **first assessment in 2024**.

### Assessment at a Glance

| **Assessment** | **Format** | **SL** | **HL** | **Weighting** |
|----------------|------------|--------|--------|---------------|
| **Paper 1** | Case study-based exam | 1h 30min | 1h 30min | SL: 35%, HL: 25% |
| **Paper 2** | Core themes exam | 1h 30min | 1h 30min | SL: 35%, HL: 30% |
| **Paper 3** | Social enterprise case study | N/A | 1h 15min | HL: 25% |
| **Internal Assessment** | Written commentary (SL) / Research project (HL) | Various | Various | SL: 30%, HL: 20% |

strengths: Identifies âchangeâ as the key concept
Provides basic organisational context for Hampton by Hilton PoznaÅ
weaknesses: Fails to integrate any change theory or framework beyond naming the concept
Analysis of change is superficial and not woven consistently throughout
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The work shows only a superficial awareness of the key concept with no meaningful theoretical application, meriting a low band score. Although the hotel context is described, the analysis lacks integrated conceptual depth, aligning with a 1/5.

strengths: Cites at least one piece of primary data (small survey)
weaknesses: More than five documents push quantity into the lowest band
Most sources are generic and offer limited company-specific depth
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Exceeding five documents without strong company-specific evidence places this in the lowest quantity band (1 mark), and the relevance is only marginally improved by a small survey, so a 1/4 is appropriate.

strengths: Applies theories descriptively to specific hotel practices
weaknesses: Lacks broader business frameworks or quantitative HR tools
Application remains largely descriptive, with relevance not always explicit
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The student uses a reasonable range of motivation theories (band 2) and applies them to examples, but the absence of wider frameworks or quantitative tools and only adequate relevance justify 2/4.
feedback.groupName: Selection and Application of Tools and Theories

strengths: Some analysis of how benefits influence motivation
Good costâbenefit insight on food provision
Effective connection from gym membership to productivity
weaknesses: Data selection is superficial (only ten-response survey, no performance metrics)
Analysis lacks depth, counter-arguments and synthesis across theories
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: With only a limited data set and one-dimensional arguments, the work sits at a 2/5. There is some analytical thinking, but it does not sustain effective integration or thorough evaluation.

strengths: Conclusion synthesizes main findings and revisits key theories
weaknesses: Recommendations are general and not tightly mapped to the research question
Link between survey findings and final recommendations is asserted rather than demonstrated
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The conclusion offers a partial, consistent answer to the research question but lacks a fully justified, explicit resolution, meriting 2/3.

strengths: Logical numbered sections and clear table of contents
weaknesses: Some lengthy section labels disrupt readability
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Overall structure is present but has noticeable inconsistencies in numbering and heading clarity, placing it at 1/2.

strengths: All required IA elements are included (title, RQ, word count, references)
weaknesses: Formatting errors in paragraphs (abrupt end, missing text)
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: While the student includes all mandatory elements, formatting and proofreading lapses prevent a seamless presentation, fitting band 1/2.

strandSelections.reasoning: The IA follows a logical structure with numbered sections, sub-headings, contents page, bibliography, and clear flow. While transitions are sometimes abrupt, the overall organisation is appropriate for the task and meets the descriptors for the highest band in this criterion.

Most documents are generic web pages explaining theories (Maslow, Herzberg, Vroom) or corporate marketing material about Hilton benefits. They are relevant but add limited depth because they do not provide rich, company-specific data. Only one piece of primary dataâa small 10-response surveyâis included, so the overall evidential depth remains superficial.

All required IA elements appear to be present: title, research question, word count, analytical sections, conclusion, references, and appendices with figures. Therefore, the work meets the requirements of the top band for presentation.

The IA cites at least seven separate sources in the bibliography and embeds multiple images/figures. According to the rubric, providing more than five documents places the work in the 1-mark band for quantity of supporting documents.

Each theory is briefly applied to a specific Hampton practice (e.g., discounts as motivators under Herzberg, flexible hours under Theory Y). The applications are relevant but remain descriptive, and links to the research question are not always explicit or sustained. This fits ârelevance not always clear but adequately applied,â rather than the higher band that requires clear and effective application throughout.

Data use is confined to descriptive details of current benefits and one bar-chart summarising ten survey responses. No financial, productivity, or HR performance data are gathered or analysed. Selection is therefore superficial and only partially supports the discussion.

The student comments on how each benefit influences motivation and occasionally links this back to the research question, demonstrating some analysis. Nonetheless, arguments are mostly one-dimensional, lack counter-arguments, and show minimal synthesis across theories, so the work does not reach the âmostly effectiveâ or âthoroughâ descriptors.

The student does provide an answerâthat Hampton should introduce more specific, ongoing motivational programmesâbut this answer is general and not fully justified by analysis or data. Hence it represents only a partial response rather than an explicit, well-supported resolution of the research question.

The student selects four established motivation theories, which is an adequate range for the research question. However, no quantitative HR tools (e.g., labour-turnover ratios, absenteeism figures) or wider BM frameworks (SWOT, STEEPLE) are employed, so the selection cannot be considered âeffectiveâ in breadth or sophistication.

The conclusion reiterates key findings and avoids clear contradiction with the limited evidence provided, but the supporting evidence base is weak. The link between survey findings and final recommendations is asserted rather than demonstrated, so consistency is only partial.

The student supplies relevant background on Hampton by Hilton PoznaÅ and links most examples (meals, flexible hours, discounts) directly to that hotel. Basic analytical commentsâe.g., classifying benefits as hygiene or motivator factorsâare clearly tied to the chosen organisation. However, integration is limited to isolated sections and is not woven consistently throughout the assessment, so the analysis does not reach the âstrong/fully integratedâ descriptors.

The student identifies âchangeâ as the key concept but then focuses almost entirely on employee motivation. Apart from naming the concept in the title, there is no discussion of how change theory frames the issue, nor how motivating staff represents or drives organisational change. Consequently, the work shows only a superficial awareness of the concept with no meaningful application or analytical integration.

annotations.comment: The title is concise and clearly identifies the IA type, fulfilling presentation requirements.
The student identifies âChangeâ as the key concept but fails to integrate any change theories or frameworks into the analysis beyond naming it, limiting conceptual depth.
The research question is clearly stated, specific, and aligns well with the organizational focus, providing a solid foundation for the investigation.
Section numbering jumps (e.g., two sections labeled page 5), suggesting a review of page references for consistency.
The introduction provides clear context about Hampton by Hilton Poznan, effectively setting the scene.
The descriptive background on the hotel lacks connection to the change concept; link these details explicitly to organizational change challenges.
The discussion of employee motivation is generic; incorporate theory or data to evaluate specific motivation drivers at Hampton.
Maslowâs theory description is too brief and the breakfast example is unclear; explain how it addresses different need levels.
annotations.groupName: Selection and Application of Tools and Theories

annotations.comment: The Vroom section omits the formula $$Motivation = Expectancy \times Instrumentality \times Valence$$; include it to deepen theory application.
annotations.groupName: Selection and Application of Tools and Theories

annotations.comment: The table of contents is comprehensive and logically organized, aiding navigation through the IA.
Some section labels are lengthy and could be more concise to improve readability of the contents page.
The costâbenefit insight regarding food provision procurement adds depth to analysis, demonstrating good evaluation of resources.
Pairing food provision with recognition programs is a strong suggestion; consider adding potential implementation steps.
Suggest quantifying the proposed performance bonuses under Vroomâs framework to demonstrate feasibility and impact.
annotations.groupName: Selection and Application of Tools and Theories

annotations.comment: The survey results figure is useful but lacks axis labels, a clear legend, and sampleâsize annotation.
Herzberg application lists hygiene and motivators but offers no critical evaluation of their impact on change initiatives.
annotations.groupName: Selection and Application of Tools and Theories

annotations.comment: The McGregor section explains X and Y but does not evaluate how each theory influences organizational change processes.
The break and food provisions section outlines practice but lacks any data on actual employee uptake or satisfaction metrics.
Gym membership analysis is detailed and connects wellbeing to productivity, illustrating effective theory application.
The mental wellness support paragraph ends abruptly with formatting errors; proofread to ensure clarity and completeness.
The summary of survey preferences links data to motivational themes effectively, reinforcing analytical insights.
Consider discussing the surveyâs reliability given its small sample size to bolster the validity of conclusions.
The conclusion synthesizes the main findings and revisits key theories, enhancing coherence.
Recommendations remain general; strengthen the conclusion by mapping specific actions directly back to the research question.
Over five sources are cited in the bibliography; prioritize depth and primary data over quantity to meet rubric depth requirements.
Citation of ChatGPT is not appropriate as a primary academic source; rely on peer-reviewed literature or primary data.
